The goal is to transform the initial state of a string of nodes into a specific target configuration.
The domain involves rotating a string of nodes.
The rotation process consists of three main actions:
1. `rotate`: Initiates the rotation at a specific node `?n` with a rotation `?r`. It updates the heading of `?n` and sets up the first pass cursor `node-first-pass-next`.
2. `rotate-first-pass`: Iterates through the connected nodes starting from the one after `?n`. It updates the heading of the next node based on the rotation and the current heading, and moves the cursor. It also clears the `at` and `heading` facts of the node being processed (temporarily).
3. `rotate-first-pass-end`: Marks the end of the first pass when the end node is reached. It transitions to the second pass cursor `node-second-pass-next`.
4. `rotate-second-pass`: Iterates through the connected nodes starting from the one after `?n` (in the second pass). It sets the `at` coordinates of the nodes based on the new headings and the previous node's coordinates. It moves the cursor.
5. `rotate-second-pass-end`: Marks the end of the second pass when the end node is reached. It clears the `rotating` flag.
